concept.headline: if is an expression in Rust — it produces a value you can bind to a variable.
concept.explanation: Rust's if checks a bool condition and runs one branch. Because if is an expression (not just a statement), you can use it where a value is expected — like the right side of a let. The match expression pattern-matches a value against a set of arms and must cover every possible case; the compiler rejects non-exhaustive matches.
keyPoints: Condition must be bool — no implicit integer conversion
Both branches of an if expression must produce the same type
match arms are evaluated top to bottom; only the first match runs
_ is the wildcard arm — must appear last if present
match must be exhaustive — all possible values must be covered

demo.intro: Watch how Rust evaluates a condition and selects a branch — then uses match for multi-way dispatch.

explanation.why: score is the value we will branch on — it drives both the if chain and the match
explanation.intent: Establish the input for conditional logic
explanation.misconception: score is immutable — the branches read it but do not change it

explanation.why: 75 is not ≥ 90, so this branch is skipped
explanation.change: condition false — skip to else if
explanation.misconception: Rust evaluates each branch's condition in order — if the first is false it moves to the next, not to all of them

explanation.why: 75 ≥ 70, so this branch runs and produces the value 'B'
explanation.misconception: Once a true branch is found, Rust does not evaluate the remaining else if conditions — short-circuit evaluation

explanation.what: Binding grade = "B" (result of the if expression)
explanation.why: The if expression evaluated to the &str "B" from the matching branch
explanation.intent: Capture the result of conditional logic into a variable
explanation.misconception: Both the if and else branches must produce the same type — mixing &str with i32 would be a compile error

explanation.why: grade holds 'B', which is formatted into the template string
explanation.misconception: println! borrows grade — it is still usable after this line

explanation.why: grade is 'B', which matches the second arm exactly
explanation.intent: Dispatch behaviour based on the grade value using pattern matching
explanation.misconception: match is not a loop — it runs exactly one arm and exits

explanation.why: The match arm for 'B' was selected and its expression executed
explanation.intent: Show the student feedback matching the grade
explanation.misconception: The _ wildcard arm would have fired if grade were anything other than 'A' or 'B' — it is a catch-all, not a fallback for errors

prediction.explanation: 5 % 2 == 1, not 0, so the condition is false and the else branch runs, producing 'odd'. println! then prints odd.

explanation.afterDemo: The if chain evaluated each condition in order until score >= 70 matched, and bound 'B' to grade. Then match dispatched to the correct arm. Neither construct required a default break — match is exhaustive by design and if short-circuits at the first true condition.
keyTakeaways: if is an expression — it produces a value that can be assigned.
All arms of an if expression must return the same type.
match must cover every possible value — the compiler checks this.

practice.prompt: Write code that checks if a number `n` (set to 17) is divisible by 3 using if/else, and prints "divisible" or "not divisible".

feedback.successMessage: Correct! 17 % 3 = 2, which is not 0, so the else branch runs.
retryHints: The modulo operator is % in Rust — n % 3 gives the remainder when n is divided by 3.
== 0 checks if the remainder is zero (divisible).
Expected output for n = 17 is 'not divisible'.

summary.title: Control Flow — Complete
takeaways: if/else is an expression — assign its result directly to a variable.
Condition must be a bool — no implicit integer-to-bool conversion.
match is exhaustive — the compiler rejects unhandled cases.
